metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether the 13 ORPHAN_EVENTs are for KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (CREATE/STATUS/COMMENT/etc.) rather than ENQUEUE_ENRICH — since fsck.py:313 already skips non-KNOWN-types, the stated version-skew false-positive mechanism cannot produce ORPHAN_EVENT reports; revise AC3's 'note the false-positive mechanism' to reflect the actual cause (e.g. a concurrent compaction that ran before ENQUEUE_ENRICH events landed, interacting with KNOWN-type events).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the 13 ORPHAN_EVENTs are plausibly 'false positives from binary/store version skew (the older binary cannot interpret the newer events' effects, so replay bookkeeping looks orphaned)' — but the fsck ORPHAN_EVENT check at fsck.py:313 already explicitly skips any event whose type is not in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES. ENQUEUE_ENRICH is in _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES (not KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES) at v0.7.1, so a v0.7.1 binary would already NOT flag ENQUEUE_ENRICH files as ORPHAN_EVENT. The stated false-positive mechanism is not supported by the code: the 13 orphans must be events of types that ARE in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES, and version skew from ENQUEUE_ENRICH cannot explain them via the stated path. The plan's AC3 ('note the false-positive mechanism here and close') may record a causally incorrect explanation if the findings clear.
findings.location: Plan body: 'false positives from binary/store version skew (the older binary cannot interpret the newer events' effects, so replay bookkeeping looks orphaned)'; AC item 'If findings clear: note the false-positive mechanism here and close'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, replace 'note the false-positive mechanism here' with a more precise frame: first confirm the event types of the 13 orphans (they must be in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES to appear as ORPHAN_EVENT); then trace whether a compaction raced with the new write session, capturing a snapshot that pre-dated the orphaned KNOWN-type events, rather than attributing the orphans to ENQUEUE_ENRICH version skew.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the ENQUEUE_ENRICH-introducing change is already present in main (check _version.py _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES); if so, update AC1 to remove the merge-gate and mark the re-audit immediately actionable.
findings.finding: AC1's gating condition ('After the ENQUEUE_ENRICH-introducing change merges to main') appears already satisfied: ENQUEUE_ENRICH, CLAIM_ENRICH, and DONE_ENRICH are present in _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES in the current main binary (_version.py:44-54), so is_unknown_newer_type() already returns False for these types and fsck already exempts them from ORPHAN_EVENT flagging. The plan treats this change as still-pending, which may make AC1's gate stale.
findings.suggested_fix: Verify `ENQUEUE_ENRICH in rebar.reducer._version._NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` on the current main HEAD. If True, restate AC1 as: 'Run `rebar fsck` from the current main binary against the live store and record the counts on this ticket' (no merge prerequisite).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend the plan body and AC3 to specify the indirect causal mechanism by which ENQUEUE_ENRICH version-skew could produce ORPHAN_EVENTs on KNOWN event types (e.g., via HLC advancement pushing KNOWN-type events behind a snapshot horizon), so that post-upgrade classification is grounded in the actual fsck logic (fsck.py:313 skips non-KNOWN types from ORPHAN_EVENT) rather than the simplified 'older binary can't interpret ENQUEUE_ENRICH effects' framing.
findings.finding: The plan's hypothesis (a) — that ENQUEUE_ENRICH version-skew is the likely cause of the 13 ORPHAN_EVENTs — is mechanically incomplete. The ORPHAN_EVENT check in fsck.py:313 already skips any event type NOT in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES, meaning unknown/newer types like ENQUEUE_ENRICH would be silently passed over, not flagged. The 13 ORPHAN_EVENTs must therefore involve KNOWN_EVENT_TYPE events appearing pre-snapshot, and any ENQUEUE_ENRICH involvement is at most indirect (e.g., concurrent ENQUEUE_ENRICH writes advancing HLC timestamps so a subsequent KNOWN-type event lands pre-snapshot). Without this causal pathway spelled out, AC3 ('note the false-positive mechanism here and close') risks being fulfilled by a superficial explanation — or, worse, genuine KNOWN-type orphans are dismissed as version-skew artifacts.
findings.location: AC3: 'If findings clear: note the false-positive mechanism here and close'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan body and AC3, note: 'The ORPHAN_EVENT check (fsck.py:313) already skips non-KNOWN event types, so the 13 ORPHAN_EVENTs are on KNOWN-type events. The likely indirect path: ENQUEUE_ENRICH writes from the concurrent session advanced the HLC clock, causing subsequent KNOWN-type events (STATUS/EDIT/etc.) to be written with timestamps that land before a snapshot compacted by the older binary, making them appear as pre-snapshot orphans. If this is confirmed after upgrade, note this mechanism and verify no KNOWN-type events were silently dropped.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'consider a follow-up' in AC3 with a stated default: either 'file a follow-up ticket titled X with the false-positive mechanism noted in its description' (committed action) or 'out of scope for this ticket — document mechanism in comments only' (explicit deferral), so the criterion has an observable done-state.
findings.finding: AC3 contains 'consider a follow-up so fsck labels skew-suspect findings when unknown-newer event types are present' — this is a placeholder that defers a real design decision (whether/how to enhance fsck) to the executor rather than making a plan decision or explicitly deferring it to a named child ticket.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'consider a follow-up so fsck labels skew-suspect findings when unknown-newer event types are present'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3's 'consider a follow-up' clause with: 'File a follow-up ticket: "fsck: annotate skew-suspect findings when store contains unknown-newer event types" and link it here.' This converts the placeholder into a concrete, in-session deliverable (a filed ticket).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete evidence format to AC2, e.g.: 'Paste a table in the ticket with columns: event UUID | finding type | classification (skew/real) | evidence (e.g., event type present in new binary's schema)' so classification completeness is verifiable.
findings.finding: AC2 ('classify each (version-skew artifact vs real orphan), and remediate real ones via A3 tooling') specifies no concrete proving command or artifact format for the classification step, so there is no evidence-producing check that an executor (or reviewer) can run to verify that classification was performed and is complete.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'classify each (version-skew artifact vs real orphan)'

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving step to AC2: after running `fsck --repair`, re-run `rebar fsck` from the current binary and record the output; require the ORPHAN_EVENT and MISSING_CREATE counts for real (non-skew) findings to be zero before closing.
findings.finding: AC2 describes remediating real orphans via 'fsck --repair' but does not include an explicit post-repair re-run of `rebar fsck` to confirm the findings are cleared — the only explicit re-run mentioned (AC4) covers `ensures` convergence, not the ORPHAN_EVENT / MISSING_CREATE counts.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'After repair, re-run `rebar fsck` and confirm ORPHAN_EVENT count for real-orphan findings = 0 and MISSING_CREATE count = 0; paste output to ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-repair step (e.g., store snapshot or backup) and a corresponding rollback/restore procedure before the `rebar fsck --repair` invocation in AC#4, so the destructive operation is reversible.
findings.finding: `fsck --repair` against the live store is a destructive/irreversible operation, but the plan specifies no rollback or back-out path (e.g., store snapshot/backup before repair). Acknowledging the upgrade-first requirement is necessary but insufficient — T4 requires an explicit reversibility mechanism.
findings.location: AC#4 and body paragraph: 'Do NOT run `rebar fsck --repair` from an older binary...'
findings.suggested_fix: Insert a step before AC#4: 'Before running `fsck --repair`, snapshot/backup the live store (e.g., `cp -r <store_path> <store_path>.bak.$(date +%s)`) and record the backup location on this ticket. If repair produces unexpected results, restore from backup before retrying.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-repair checkpoint (e.g., store snapshot or export) and document the recovery procedure if `fsck --repair` fails or is interrupted mid-run.
findings.finding: The plan specifies running `rebar fsck --repair` against a live store but includes no pre-repair backup/snapshot step and no recovery path if the repair partially completes or fails mid-run, leaving the store in an inconsistent state.
findings.location: AC line 4 / body paragraph: 'Do NOT run `rebar fsck --repair` from an older binary'
findings.suggested_fix: Before the `--repair` run, take a point-in-time snapshot or export of the live store (e.g., `rebar store export --output pre-repair-$(date +%Y%m%d).bak`). Record the snapshot location on this ticket. If `--repair` exits non-zero or the post-run fsck count is unexpected, restore from snapshot rather than re-running repair blindly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit precondition to the repair step: confirm all writers to the live store are running the ENQUEUE_ENRICH-capable binary (or that no concurrent write sessions are active) before executing `rebar fsck --repair`, and document how to verify this.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify that all writers to the shared live store must be on the same (merged) binary version — or that the store is quiesced — before `rebar fsck --repair` is executed. A concurrent session on unmerged/different code writing ENQUEUE_ENRICH events during the repair window creates the same TOCTOU race the plan's background section already describes, potentially corrupting the repair outcome or regenerating false-positive orphan counts.
findings.location: Background section ('concurrent session on unmerged code') and AC line 4 ('ensures converged via rebar fsck --repair run from the current binary')
findings.suggested_fix: Before AC4's --repair step, add a checklist item: 'Verify zero concurrent write sessions on non-merged binaries (e.g., check running rebar instances / session list); proceed only when all writers report binary >= X.Y.Z (the merged version).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the expected idempotency behavior of `rebar fsck --repair` (safe to re-run vs. one-shot) and add a recovery step for partial-completion (e.g., re-run is safe because X, or restore from snapshot Y if aborted).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how a partial or mid-run failure of `rebar fsck --repair` should be handled. If --repair is interrupted after modifying some records but before converging ensures to 5/5, the store is left in an intermediate state; the plan provides no recovery or idempotency guarantee for re-running --repair against a partially-repaired store.
findings.location: AC line 4: '`ensures` converged (5/5 applied) via `rebar fsck --repair` run from the current binary'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-repair safety step: assert that `fsck --repair` is idempotent (re-runnable without double-applying), document the store state after a partial failure, and specify a back-out path (e.g., store snapshot/backup before repair, or compensating-forward procedure) so DATA LOSS RISK on partial failure is explicitly eliminated.
findings.finding: The plan invokes `rebar fsck --repair` against a live store but specifies no back-out path if the repair fails mid-run. The schema/linkage state after a partial failure is never asserted, leaving the store in an unknown intermediate state with no described recovery procedure.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 4: '`ensures` converged (5/5 applied) via `rebar fsck --repair` run from the current binary'
findings.suggested_fix: Before invoking `fsck --repair`, snapshot or checkpoint the live store (e.g., `cp -a <store_path> <store_path>.pre-repair-$(date +%s)`). Document that re-running `fsck --repair` on the same store is idempotent (each finding check is a read-before-write guard). If idempotency is not guaranteed by the tool, add an AC requiring a dry-run (`fsck` without `--repair`) immediately before the repair run to confirm the finding set is stable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `fsck --repair` must be run with the store quiesced (no concurrent writers) or whether it is safe under live writes; if live writes are permitted, specify that a follow-up `rebar fsck` (read-only) is required immediately after repair to confirm zero findings before closing the ticket.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the dual-write window: new events can be written to the live store during the `fsck --repair` run (the plan itself acknowledges concurrent sessions exist), and there is no described mechanism to ensure events written during repair are not themselves immediately orphaned or missed by the repair pass.
findings.location: Plan body — 'WARNing the store contains ENQUEUE_ENRICH events newer than the running binary (written by a concurrent session on unmerged code)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
